paragraphs: I am an observational astronomer and Research Fellow at the University of Warwick, working on compact interacting binaries — systems where a neutron star, black hole, or white dwarf accretes material from a stellar companion. My overarching goal is to understand how these extreme systems exchange mass and energy with their environment through outflows (disc winds and jets), and how this feedback governs their observable phenomenology and long-term evolution.

To address these questions, I design and execute time-critical, coordinated multi-wavelength campaigns spanning the far-ultraviolet to the radio, using space- and ground-based facilities including the James Webb and Hubble Space Telescopes, XMM-Newton, VLT, Gemini, VLA, and MeerKAT. I combine high-resolution spectroscopy with broad-band coverage and time-domain photometry to build an empirical, physically motivated picture of the accretion flow and its coupling to winds and jets — confronting observations with photoionisation and radiative-transfer modelling to extract physical parameters rather than qualitative labels.

I completed my PhD at the University of Southampton (2017–2022) under Prof. Christian Knigge, where I led the first strictly simultaneous UV/optical/X-ray/radio observing campaign of a low-mass X-ray binary, resulting in a first-author paper in Nature. Since then, I have led multiple successful multi-facility campaigns totalling over 190 hours of telescope time and 65 HST orbits awarded as lead investigator. I have published 67 refereed papers (6 as first author, 11 as second or third author, including works in Nature and Nature Astronomy), with an h-index of 17 and over 1,000 citations.

Beyond observations, I am deeply invested in instrumentation and research software. I co-led an STFC/UKRI grant (£265,000) to build a high-speed multi-beam camera for time-domain astrophysics, and I serve as Instrument Scientist within the OPTICAM collaboration, a triple-channel optical system designed to explore the fastest timescales in astronomy. I am also a member of the HiPERCAM collaboration and sit on proposal review panels for JWST, HST, Swift, and GTC. I actively mentor PhD and MSc students, and I am committed to making astronomical software and analysis pipelines more accessible and reproducible.

research.title: Outflows from Accreting Compact Objects
body: Disc winds and jets are ubiquitous wherever accretion discs form — from stellar-mass neutron stars and black holes in X-ray binaries to supermassive black holes at the centres of galaxies. In low-mass X-ray binaries (LMXBs), these outflows carry away mass, energy, and angular momentum, regulating accretion state transitions, shaping the long-term orbital evolution of the binary, and potentially triggering star formation in the surrounding interstellar medium. Despite their clear importance, even the fundamental question of what drives these winds — thermal heating, radiation pressure, or magnetic forces — remains open.

My research addresses this directly. During my PhD, I coordinated the first strictly simultaneous UV/optical/X-ray/radio observing campaign of an accreting neutron-star transient, Swift J1858.6−0814, revealing a persistent, multi-phase disc wind present across all spectral states — a result published in Nature (Castro Segura et al. 2022). This established multi-phase outflows as a key mode of feedback and demonstrated that winds are not confined to the soft X-ray states as previously thought. Building on this, I have led large multi-facility campaigns with JWST, HST, XMM-Newton, VLT, VLA, and NICER, sampling a range of accretion states, inclinations, and compact object types (neutron stars, black holes, and white dwarfs) to disentangle the contributions of different driving mechanisms.

A defining aspect of my approach is the use of forward modelling — photoionisation and Monte Carlo radiative-transfer calculations — to move beyond qualitative spectral classifications and directly recover wind mass-loss rates, velocities, ionisation structures, and kinetic luminosities. My submitted study of the bright black hole transient Swift J1727.8−1613 exemplifies this: combining optical, UV, and X-ray data spanning the full outburst, I track the disc response to jet ejections and the late-outburst emergence of powerful optical disc winds, connecting the accretion–ejection cycle to wind phenomenology in a state-resolved way. My medium-term goal is to deliver mechanism-aware, state-dependent wind prescriptions that can be directly incorporated into binary-evolution and population-synthesis codes — providing the calibrated angular-momentum loss terms still missing in current models.
research.imageAlt: Artist's impression of an accretion disc wind driven from a compact binary system
research.imageCaption: Artist's impression of a powerful disc wind driven from the inner accretion disc of a compact object. Such outflows are present across all scales of accreting systems, from stellar-mass binaries to supermassive black holes.

research.title: White Dwarf Pulsars: A New Class of Compact Binary
body: White dwarf pulsars are a newly identified and remarkably rare class of compact binary in which the strongly magnetised, rapidly spinning white dwarf primary drives pulsed, broad-band emission — from the optical to the radio — that cannot be explained by accretion alone, but instead requires the spin-down of the white dwarf itself. First identified with the discovery of AR Scorpii in 2016, these systems probe magnetospheric particle acceleration in compact binaries, provide laboratories for studying the origin and evolution of magnetic fields in white dwarfs, and connect a wide range of related objects — intermediate polars, polars, long-period radio transients, and type Ia supernova progenitors — through a common evolutionary thread.

I identified and characterised the third confirmed white dwarf pulsar, SDSS J230641.47+244055.8, establishing an observational 'blueprint' for the class: a set of measurable signatures — coherent pulsations, a characteristic spectral energy distribution, non-thermal optical polarisation, and spin-period evolution — that future surveys should target to convert a 'candidate' into an 'evolutionary constraint' (Castro Segura et al. 2025, MNRAS, highlighted in Nature Astronomy). Using OPTICAM, I also revealed the hidden spin signal in the WZ Sge-type system GOTO 065054.49+593624.51, demonstrating that high-speed photometry can expose the 'dynamical clock' linking accretion geometry and magnetic interaction even in systems not previously classified as pulsars (Castro Segura et al. 2025, MNRAS Letters).

My immediate programme is to move from exceptional individual discoveries to a population-level understanding. I have identified a fourth white dwarf pulsar candidate (unpublished) and secured targeted radio follow-up with the VLA for two new systems. I am also actively involved in a Gaia-based photometric and spectroscopic search for additional candidates, which provides a well-defined selection function for eventual demographic inference. In the medium term, I aim to build a statistically meaningful sample of tens of systems that enables robust correlations between pulsating behaviour, binary parameters, and evolutionary state — turning white dwarf pulsars into quantitative probes of magnetic torques, angular-momentum evolution, and the evolutionary pathways linking magnetic compact binaries.

research.title: Binary Evolution and the Role of Outflows
body: The long-term evolution of compact interacting binaries is governed by the rates at which mass and angular momentum are lost from the system. Disc winds and jets are not merely spectroscopic curiosities — they are the primary channel through which accreting systems shed angular momentum and regulate mass transfer, directly shaping the orbital periods, mass-transfer rates, and ultimately the endpoints (type Ia supernovae, gravitational-wave sources, or detached double degenerates) of compact binaries. Yet current binary-evolution and population-synthesis models almost universally adopt simplified, empirical prescriptions for angular-momentum loss that were not derived from physically motivated wind models.

A central objective of my research programme is to bridge this gap. By combining state-resolved disc-wind diagnostics with forward modelling across a sample of compact binaries spanning different compact-object masses, mass-transfer rates, orbital inclinations, and accretion states, I aim to deliver a public library of mechanism-tagged, state-dependent wind and angular-momentum-loss prescriptions — ready for direct incorporation into evolutionary codes. My study of Swift J1858.6−0814 (Castro Segura et al. 2024, MNRAS) exemplifies the approach: far-UV spectroscopy in three accretion states simultaneously constrained the donor-star properties and the evolutionary track of the system, connecting observable diagnostics to evolutionary state in a way that individual X-ray or optical datasets alone cannot achieve.

In the longer term, I aim to connect stellar-mass laboratories to the broader multi-messenger landscape. The compact binaries I study are the progenitor systems for gravitational-wave sources detectable by LISA in the 2030s, and calibrated wind-driven angular-momentum loss prescriptions are essential for robust LISA-era population forecasts. I will also test whether the accretion–ejection scaling relations measured in stellar-mass systems extend to the AGN regime, linking compact-binary physics to galaxy-scale feedback and reinforcing the unique value of X-ray binaries as laboratories for accretion physics across all mass scales.
research.imageAlt: Artist's impression of the Swift J1727.8-1613 black hole X-ray binary system during its 2023-2024 outburst
research.imageCaption: Artist's impression of Swift J1727.8−1613, one of the brightest black hole X-ray transients of recent years, during its 2023–2024 outburst. Multi-wavelength monitoring of such events provides the state-resolved disc–wind–jet datasets needed to constrain binary-evolution models.
research.imageCredits: Credit: Instituto de Astrofísica de Canarias (IAC)

research.title: Instrumentation and Research Software
body: In the era of wide-field time-domain surveys, the bottleneck for compact-binary science is rarely discovery — it is rapid, high-quality follow-up and reproducible analysis. I treat instrumentation and software as first-class research outputs, because they multiply scientific return: a well-engineered pipeline or a robustly characterised instrument enables faster validation, lowers the barrier to student contributions, and accelerates publication across the collaboration.

I co-led an STFC/UKRI grant (£265,000) to design and build a high-speed multi-beam camera for time-domain astrophysics, and I have served as Lead Scientist and Instrument Scientist within the OPTICAM collaboration — a triple-channel optical system developed in partnership with the Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM), designed to obtain simultaneous light curves in three optical bands at sub-second cadence. I led quality control, pipeline development, and the scientific exploitation of OPTICAM data, including results published in MNRAS and MNRAS Letters. I am also a member of the HiPERCAM collaboration (PI: Vik Dhillon), one of the world's fastest astronomical cameras, which I use for high-cadence follow-up of compact binaries.

On the software side, I am developing a modernised, user-friendly toolkit for binary parameter inference — building on the powerful but ageing modelling code developed by the late Tom Marsh. The goal is to provide modular, well-tested, and openly documented components for radial-velocity and light-curve modelling, lowering the barrier to robust binary characterisation for researchers and students at all career stages. I am also co-author of the open-source opticam Python package for astronomical image reduction (Irving, Castro Segura et al., submitted to RAS Techniques and Instruments), and I actively develop rapid-response triggering pipelines that link survey alert streams to coordinated multi-facility follow-up of transient outbursts.
